Smithsonian National Museum of American History Director Anthea Hartig faced intense questioning from lawmakers during two Capitol Hill hearings this week. The hearings followed a White House Domestic Policy Council report accusing the museum of using taxpayer dollars and its status as a world-renowned institution to advance a "radical activist ideology."

During a House hearing Tuesday, Heritage Foundation expert Mike Gonzalez testified that a multiyear special exhibit, "The Electric Dr. Franklin," included a display that "pondered whether he had ever done experiments, electrical electroshock on indentured servants or slaves." 

WATCH: SMITHSONIAN DIRECTOR PRESSED ON WOKE EXHIBITS: ‘IS MICKEY MOUSE RACIST?’

Gonzalez testified that the display included this suggestion "without producing any evidence whatsoever," which he called "shocking."

"Why smear a Founding Father such as Ben Franklin by pondering such a really bizarre thing," Gonzalez asked.

Immediately after Gonzalez’s testimony, Rep. Brian Jack, R-Ga., confronted Hartig, saying, "We've heard a lot of concerning examples as just referenced. Do you have any regrets in your tenure thus far [about] some of the exhibits that have been displayed? Do you regret any of the exhibits and, if so, which?"

Hartig answered, "I am incredibly proud of the work we've done, but we are imperfect, and we surely have made mistakes. But I don't have any regrets."

Jack pressed further, asking, "Which mistakes would you say you've made?"

"There are always factual errors," Hartig responded.

"One of my favorite, currently," she added, "is that one of our volunteers has pointed out that we've been mislabeling a coin in the American Presidency exhibit for years, and we were, of course, you know, slightly embarrassed about that, but we fixed it."

Hartig became director of the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History in February 2019. The Benjamin Franklin exhibit opened to the public in 2021 and remained on display through 2025.

'THE VIEW' CO-HOST WHINES SMITHSONIAN HEARINGS ARE ABOUT TRUMP ADMINISTRATION NOT WANTING TO PAY REPARATIONS

The museum has a webpage containing expanded material from the exhibit. The site states that "researchers also used people as conductors to determine how far an electrical charge might travel, and if electrical effects would fade with distance. 

"Franklin wrote about experiments in which he took shocks and used family and friends in these ways, although he seldom identified who in particular assisted him. Indentured servants and enslaved people in his household could also have been used, although we may never know for certain."

A sweeping new White House report concluded that the Smithsonian Institution’s National Museum of American History has become a taxpayer-backed institution of "ideological capture" and "extreme political activism."

According to the 162-page "Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History" report, delivered on America’s 250th birthday, NMAH treats the American story as a political tool rather than a shared national inheritance.

The White House Domestic Policy Council wrote that the "Smithsonian Institution, and the National Museum of American History in particular, under its current leadership and current interpretive ideology, cannot be trusted to tell America’s story honestly and in a way that is inspiring, unifying, and worthy of our great republic."

Further, the report said that "by the intention and at the direction of current Museum and Smithsonian leadership, NMAH has become subject to institutional capture by a radical, activist ideology that is fundamentally opposed to telling the noble, honest story of the great country we know and love."

WATCH: SMITHSONIAN HISTORY MUSEUM DIRECTOR STUMBLES OVER BASIC QUESTIONS ON SEX: ‘I’M NOT SURE’

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

During her testimony Tuesday, Hartig said "the Smithsonian takes the concerns raised in the White House Domestic Policy Counsel's report seriously, and as we continue to fact-check the report, we unwaveringly attest that it does not fairly or accurately characterize the full body of work at the museum."

She added that "there is always room for improvement, but I also know the beauty and the inspiration and the expertise that lies in our collections, our exhibits and our programming."

Fox News Digital reached out to the Smithsonian Institution, the National Museum of American History and Gonzalez for additional comment.

item.title: Vance Boelter sentenced for shootings of Minnesota lawmakers and spouses
item.description: A Minnesota man was sentenced to consecutive life terms for killing a former state House speaker and her husband, and shooting another elected official.
item.content:encoded:
Vance Luther Boelter — the man who pleaded guilty to killing a Minnesota lawmaker and her husband and opening fire on another elected official and his spouse — was sentenced Thursday to life in prison. 

Boelter, 58, received two consecutive life sentences, plus 40 years in prison, all to be served consecutively over the killing of former House Speaker Hortman and her husband Mark, as well as critically wounding state Sen. John Hoffman and his wife Yvette, FOX 9 reported. 

The sentence was part of a plea deal that allowed Boelter to avoid the death penalty. He apologized before the U.S. District Judge John R. Tunheim handed down the punishment.

FAKE COP SUSPECTED IN LAWMAKER ASSASSINATION HAD EXTENSIVE SECURITY TRAINING BEFORE 'TARGETED' ATTACK

"To every person I have caused loss, pain, heartache, confusion, sadness, shame, or loneliness, I am truly sorry," Boelter said. 

Tunheim said it was "the longest sentence I have imposed after hearing thousands of cases over the years, but I do believe it is deserved."

In court, Hoffman said he and his family were reminded of the violence of that night, to the point that they don't receive mail at home anymore, and have installed solid steel doors for protection.

"The defendant looked me in the eyes and pulled the trigger. The defendant looked at my wife and chose to do the same," Hoffman said. "My wife and I now live with injuries. We live with our bodies remembering the gunfire."

They have also changed how they do everyday tasks because of their injuries. Mark Hortman's parents and siblings voice anger at Boelter and at the justice system.

MINNESOTA SHOOTING TIMELINE: SUSPECT VANCE BOELTER'S LAST WORDS TO FAMILY BEFORE CAPTURE

"It will not offer me peace of mind that he will have room and board provided for the rest of his life, paid for by my tax dollars," said Lisa Hortman Bean, Mark's sister.

Boelter admitted he spent months stalking his targets before disguising himself as a police officer on June 14, 2025, and driving to their home and opening fire. He then exchanged gunfire with police while escaping on foot.

Boelter was arrested following a two-day manhunt. 

During Boelter’s interview with investigators, he also referenced his Christian faith while discussing his sentiments toward the victims and their loved ones. 

"You can maybe ask if somebody believes that, and they love God and that they love their neighbor – allegedly – how could they be involved in a situation where some people are no longer here that were here before?" Boelter asked, adding, "I’ll let you chew on that one." 

Democratic State Rep. Kristin Bahner was one of two other legislators whose homes Boelter visited that night, but she and her family were on vacation. 

"I could not fathom why I was spared and (Hortman) was not," she said. "You failed because I carry her with me. In my mind and in my heart, she guides me."

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

item.title: Norway soccer chief complains over Trump's FIFA involvement after US star's red card: 'This was an error'
item.description: NFF President Lise Klaveness said she will pursue a formal ethics complaint over FIFA's decision to reverse Folarin Balogun's red card ban.
item.content:encoded:
The World Cup may be over, but there are some still upset over the controversy surrounding USMNT star Folarin Balogun’s red card ban being suspended.

Norwegian Football Federation (NFF) President Lise Klaveness said she is going to ask her board to pursue a formal ethics complaint over the incident. 

She called on FIFA to acknowledge the decision to reverse Balogun’s ban as an error.

"We need our leader in FIFA to say that this was an error," Klaveness told The Times. "If you start to adapt in such a way towards state leaders and state politics, you will move your behavior and the organization's behavior and also the red line of what states' leaders should interfere with. And that happened."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Klaveness claimed the proper process was not followed and that bending the rules could put the sport at risk.

"First of all, it should not have happened. It is very important now to have honest communication around it. We all know that this verdict was influenced from external forces and did not have the proper process," Klaveness said. 

"When you bend a rule like this, you're on a slippery slope that will put the whole game at risk. It is a worry for the game when you compromise fundamental rules of the game."

Klaveness called the case of FIFA suspending Balogun’s red card ban both "serious" and "worrying."

"This case was so serious and so worrying that I hope that it might contribute to bring people out a little bit and don't fear to bring up cases," she said. 

"I will bring it to a board meeting and discuss with my board, and then we do it. To me, it should be in the same sphere of ethical complaint."

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

President Donald Trump reached out to FIFA President Gianni Infantino to ask the organization to review the decision to give U.S. soccer star Folarin Balogun a red card. The red card meant Balogun would have been suspended for the team’s knockout match against Belgium on Monday, but FIFA decided to suspend the one-game ban after Trump’s call.

Trump addressed the controversy earlier this month.

"All I did was I asked for a review because I didn’t think it was a foul," the president said. "And, again, I’m good at this stuff. I didn’t think it was a foul. I thought it was two great athletes who crashed into each other and got entangled. That was not a guy punching somebody in the face or anything that would be different.

"I think it’s a terrible … if they wouldn’t allow a top player, maybe the best, maybe among the best on the team, to play. I think it would have had a big stain. I relayed it. I didn’t tell him what to do. I don’t believe he made the decision. I think it was a committee that made the decision, and they made the right decision because, No. 1, it wasn’t a foul, and you want to see a game with your best players."

Trump said he took issue with the initial red card assessed to Balogun.

"If you would have taken him out, I think it would have really stained this incredible championship," Trump continued. "We gotta have our best players and Belgium. Belgium’s got a great team, by the way. 

"We have our best players, and they have to have their best. If we win or we lose, it’s fair. Otherwise, let’s say we lost to them, then we lost the game. It would be a terrible thing. I think they made a really brilliant decision.

"I think the referee’s call was horrible, and nobody talks about that. They talk about the red card like it’s fine. The referee’s decision to red card, I didn’t know what the hell a red card was, and when I found out I said, ‘You gotta be kidding.’ … I said, ‘Wow, that’s a lot of power, that’s terrible.’ And then I looked at his past, and it wasn’t so great."

While Balogun was able to play, the USMNT lost 4-1 to Belgium, ending their World Cup run in the Round of 16.

Fox News Digital reached out to FIFA for comment but did not immediately receive a response.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Iowa teachers suspended over viral student-run drag show reach settlement before trial
item.description: Three Ankeny school district teachers settled their discrimination lawsuit for about $150,000 after being disciplined for a student-led drag show.
item.content:encoded:
Three Iowa teachers who were disciplined for helping to facilitate a student-led drag show have reached a settlement after filing discrimination lawsuits against the Ankeny Community School District.

The district launched investigations into teachers Jill Bjorklund, Mollie Jo Blahunka and PJ Hurley in May 2022, after they approved or attended a drag show for students held on campus after school hours.

Students with the Gender and Sexuality Alliance (GSA) clubs at Ankeny High School and Southview Middle School hosted youth drag performers from the Central Iowa Youth Pride Pageant for the school clubs' final meeting of the year, according to the plaintiffs' complaint. 

COLORADO TEACHER FIRED AFTER STUDENTS ALLEGEDLY PRESSURED TO KISS CLASSMATES IN SKITS

Blahunka and Bjorklund were faculty sponsors for the school clubs, while Hurley was another teacher invited by students to attend.

According to the complaint, students voluntarily attended the event and "no one at the event was harmed or offended, nor was any educational purpose interrupted by any part of the event."

The event attracted controversy after clips of the drag performance went viral, prompting Ankeny to launch an investigation.

Ankeny Community School District said in a statement that the event was not approved by the school administration and did "not follow the correct protocols prior to the meeting."

The plaintiffs disputed this statement. In their complaint, Blahunka alleged she spoke with the Ankeny High School activities director about the students' desire to host an end-of-the-year celebration on campus in conjunction with the Southview GSA club and later reserved the high school auditorium for the event.

According to The Des Moines Register, all three teachers were put on leave after the incident, with Bjorklund and Blahunka being suspended for a few days during the next school year. 

All three employees were banned from sponsoring school activities and threatened with termination for any future violations, according to The Des Moines Register. The teachers' lawsuit also claimed the district filed ethics charges with the Iowa Board of Education, which eventually dismissed the charges.

WISCONSIN TEACHER FIRED AFTER SOCIAL MEDIA POST ADVOCATING TO 'MAKE AMERICANS GREAT ASSASSINS AGAIN'

The teachers sued, accusing the district of discrimination against their speech in support of LGBTQ students and retaliation, in violation of their First Amendment rights. The complaint also says Blahunka identifies as a queer woman, Hurley as a gay man and Bjorklund as the mother of a transgender child.

After a yearslong legal battle in both state and federal court, the two parties reached a settlement just before the case was scheduled to go to trial July 20.

According to the Register, the district agreed to pay each teacher about $27,700 plus $67,000 for attorneys' fees, totaling about $150,000.

A spokesperson for Ankeny Community School District told Fox News Digital that the matter had been "resolved to the satisfaction of all parties."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

Ankeny Community Schools and an attorney for the plaintiffs did not immediately return Fox News Digital's request for comment.

In May 2023, Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds signed education bill SF 496 into law, which critics labeled the "Don't say LGBTQ law." 

The law forbade classroom instruction on gender identity and sexual orientation for kindergarten through sixth grade students, required school staff to notify parents if a student requested to use a different name or pronoun, and banned books containing descriptions or depictions of sex acts from all K–12 school libraries.

item.title: White House defends WNBA star Sophie Cunningham after she speaks out in support of protecting women's sports
item.description: Karoline Leavitt backed Sophie Cunningham after the Indiana Fever star faced backlash for speaking out against biological men competing in women's sports.
item.content:encoded:
White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt spoke out in support of Indiana Fever star Sophie Cunningham on Thursday after Cunningham spoke out publicly in support of protecting women's sports from transgender athletes.

"Men should not be playing in women's sports. It's completely ludicrous that anyone could support that," Leavitt told reporters.

"It's even more ludicrous that you do have Sophie Cunningham, a female athlete herself, very accomplished in her own right, coming forth and speaking that truth that men should(n't) play in women's sports. The backlash she is receiving from Democrats and left-wing figures across the country is astonishing. They are clearly very out of touch with the American public on this issue, which again, is an 80/20 issue. It's common sense."

SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM DOUBLES DOWN AHEAD OF SUN GAME AFTER ESPN PROFILE STIRS CONTROVERSY: 'I SAID WHAT I SAID'

Cunningham sent shockwaves in the ongoing culture war over the WNBA and trans athletes in women's sports on Tuesday after revealing her stance on the issue to ESPN.

"I got a lot of negative feedback about me hating trans. And I'm like, 'I never once said that,'" Cunningham said.

"I think that I am here to extend love. But I also think with that love is truth, being honest. And I want to protect young girls in a locker room, or young girls in sport who shouldn't have to go against biological men."

SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM MANAGES TO ANGER BOTH THE LEFT AND RIGHT WITH A SIMPLE SOCIAL MEDIA POST

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Cunningham doubled down on her statement while speaking to reporters on Wednesday ahead of the Fever's win against the Connecticut Sun.

"I think I said what I said. I think it's kind of common sense," she said.

"I'll always believe in that. It's really important to protect children, and that's little girls who are also involved in that category... I'm not a very political person, but I do have beliefs. I have morals, and I'll always stand on those... I extend love, and I don't dislike anyone. I think there is room for absolutely everyone here."
